hazard: Anti-free-flow mechanism absent or bypassed on administration set removal
sequence_of_events: Administration set is removed from pump body without closing roller clamp → gravitational siphoning forces rapid uncontrolled drug delivery to patient
hazardous_situation: Patient exposed to uncontrolled gravity-driven drug bolus from IV bag
harm: Rapid drug overdose; fatal for high-alert medications (opioids, insulin, vasoactive agents)
risk_control: Mandatory anti-free-flow (AFF) administration set with automatic clamp engagement on set removal; pump design incompatible with non-AFF gravity sets; AFF functionality check step required in IFU; operator training on AFF engagement confirmation
verification_method: Free-flow resistance testing per IEC 60601-2-24; simulated clinical removal testing with gloved hands; summative usability evaluation confirming AFF engagement awareness

hazard: Occlusion detection threshold set too high or downstream occlusion detector malfunction
sequence_of_events: Downstream occlusion develops gradually (kinked tubing or clotted cannula) → pressure builds below alarm threshold → drug not delivered for extended period → occlusion clears and accumulated pressure releases accumulated-dose bolus
hazardous_situation: Patient receives delayed and then excessive bolus of medication, or receives no drug for a prolonged clinical period
harm: Underdose during occlusion causing treatment failure; subsequent dose bolus causing overdose
risk_control: Upstream and downstream occlusion detection with adjustable threshold validated for high-alert medications; accumulated volume estimate displayed at alarm trigger; clinical protocol addressing occlusion resolution and dose management; automatic pump pause on alarm
verification_method: Occlusion alarm sensitivity testing per IEC 60601-2-24; accumulated volume accuracy validation; clinical simulation study with nursing staff

hazard: Air-in-line detector sensitivity below validated threshold
sequence_of_events: IV bag empties without triggering empty-bag detection → air enters administration set → air-in-line detector generates delayed or absent alert → air bolus passes through pump to IV cannula
hazardous_situation: Clinically significant air bolus passes undetected through infusion line toward patient vasculature
harm: Venous or arterial air embolism; potentially fatal pulmonary or cardiac arrest
risk_control: Ultrasonic air-in-line detector validated to detect bubbles ≥0.1 mL per IEC 60601-2-24; empty-bag detection with automatic pump stop; mandatory priming procedure before pump activation; periodic detector self-check at power-on
verification_method: Air detection sensitivity testing across validated bubble volumes and infusion rates per IEC 60601-2-24; empty-bag alarm activation testing; simulated priming evaluation in usability study

hazard: Drug library contains multiple similarly named or concentrations-ambiguous drug profiles
sequence_of_events: Clinician searches drug library under time pressure → selects incorrectly named or adjacent drug profile → programs infusion using wrong concentration or dose unit → pump calculates incorrect flow rate for prescribed dose
hazardous_situation: Patient receives drug at a dose significantly above or below the prescribed therapeutic dose due to library selection error
harm: Overdose or underdose of high-alert medication; adverse drug event or treatment failure
risk_control: Dose error reduction software (DERS) with hard minimum and maximum dose limits per drug; drug profile naming convention minimising confusion; mandatory confirmation screen displaying drug name, concentration, and calculated dose before start; independent double-check requirement for high-alert protocols
verification_method: DERS hard limit validation against drug library; summative usability evaluation with nurse participants under simulated clinical conditions; drug library validation testing against clinical formulary

hazard: Mechanical wear in peristaltic or syringe pump mechanism causing volumetric inaccuracy
sequence_of_events: Pump mechanism wears with repeated clinical use → delivered volume per actuator cycle decreases or increases → cumulative dose deviation accumulates over extended infusion period
hazardous_situation: Patient receives systematically incorrect cumulative dose over hours of infusion
harm: Chronic underdose (treatment failure) or overdose (drug toxicity) particularly for narrow therapeutic index drugs
risk_control: Flow accuracy specifications meeting IEC 60601-2-24 requirements validated at start-of-life and end-of-life; preventive maintenance schedule with recalibration intervals; annual accuracy verification protocol; service life limit after which pump must be decommissioned
verification_method: Flow accuracy testing per IEC 60601-2-24 at new and end-of-service conditions; mechanical wear accelerated life testing; field calibration validation study

hazard: Insufficient battery capacity or inadequate battery management during patient transport
sequence_of_events: Pump operates on battery during patient transport between clinical areas → battery depletes → low battery alarm is missed or insufficient response time → pump ceases infusion before reaching next AC power source
hazardous_situation: Critical drug infusion unexpectedly interrupts during patient transport with no immediate ability to restart
harm: Interruption of life-sustaining therapy (vasopressor, anticoagulant, analgesic); haemodynamic instability, pain, thrombus formation
risk_control: Battery capacity validated for minimum 4-hour transport at maximum clinical load; multi-stage low battery alarm at ≥60 and ≥30 minutes remaining; non-interruptible alarm escalation after initial silence; transport protocol requiring battery charge verification before patient movement
verification_method: Battery life testing under maximum load conditions; low battery alarm threshold validation at both warning stages; clinical transport simulation testing across representative patient transfer scenarios

hazard: Small touchscreen target areas and absence of adequate confirmation barrier for dose entry
sequence_of_events: Clinician enters dose or infusion rate on touchscreen under time pressure or wearing gloves → adjacent key activated → incorrect value accepted without sufficient re-entry confirmation step → infusion programmed at wrong rate
hazardous_situation: Patient infused at significantly incorrect rate (e.g., 10-fold error) due to data entry error on touchscreen
harm: Drug overdose or underdose; potentially fatal for high-alert medications (heparin, insulin, morphine)
risk_control: DERS hard limits blocking activation if dose exceeds validated maximum; mandatory re-entry confirmation step for any dose exceeding soft limit; touchscreen key size and spacing validated per IEC 62366; soft key lockout requiring clinician override above soft limits
verification_method: Simulated gloved touchscreen use accuracy testing; DERS hard limit activation validation; summative usability study with nurse participants under representative clinical workload

hazard: Software fault causing pump software to become unresponsive during active infusion
sequence_of_events: Runtime software fault (memory overflow, unhandled exception, watchdog timeout) occurs during active infusion → pump user interface becomes unresponsive → pump either continues at last programmed rate without responding to commands or stops abruptly
hazardous_situation: Clinician cannot modify or stop infusion; pump may deliver uncontrolled drug or cease critical therapy
harm: Drug over-delivery if pump continues uncontrolled; therapy interruption if pump stops unexpectedly; both potentially life-threatening for critical medications
risk_control: Fail-safe software architecture: on watchdog timeout or detected fault, pump stops infusion and activates highest-priority audible and visual alarm; hardware-level emergency stop accessible without software; software developed and tested per IEC 62304 Class C with fault injection testing
verification_method: Fault injection and software robustness testing per IEC 62304; watchdog timer activation and fail-safe stop validation; hardware stop mechanism functional testing; regression testing before each software release

hazard: High alarm frequency causing clinical staff to habitually silence infusion pump alarms
sequence_of_events: Pump generates frequent low-priority alarms (near-empty bag, routine KVO alerts) → clinical staff conditioned to silence alarms quickly without full assessment → high-priority occlusion or air-in-line alarm is silenced reflexively → true patient safety alarm goes unresolved
hazardous_situation: Clinical staff inadvertently silence a true critical patient safety alarm due to alarm fatigue
harm: Failure to respond to critical infusion event; patient harm from undetected drug delivery error or air embolism
risk_control: Alarm priority differentiation with distinct audible tones per IEC 60601-1-8; high-priority alarms escalate after short silence period; smart bag-empty prediction to reduce low-priority alarm frequency; alarm management clinical protocol documentation; alarm frequency data reviewed in post-market surveillance
verification_method: Alarm prioritisation validation per IEC 60601-1-8; auditory distinctiveness testing across alarm categories; alarm escalation timing validation; clinical simulation study measuring alarm fatigue behaviour

hazard: Generic or incompatible third-party administration set used with proprietary pump mechanism
sequence_of_events: User selects non-validated generic administration set → tubing diameter or material compliance differs from validated set → pump delivers incorrect volume per actuator stroke → flow rate deviates from programmed rate throughout infusion
hazardous_situation: Patient receives systematic cumulative over- or under-dose due to set dimension mismatch
harm: Cumulative overdose or underdose; clinical significance dependent on drug and magnitude of deviation
risk_control: Administration set compatibility list in IFU with validated set part numbers; physical or electronic set recognition preventing operation with non-validated sets; validated set labeling with compatible pump model reference; clinical procurement policy specifying approved sets
verification_method: Flow accuracy testing with validated and non-validated set types across infusion rates; set recognition feature validation; labeling adequacy review

hazard: Patient misidentification or incorrect therapy profile selection in multi-patient clinical environment
sequence_of_events: Clinician retrieves patient order from clinical information system → incorrect patient or therapy profile selected due to similar name or adjacent list item → infusion programmed for wrong patient's drug protocol → patient receives incorrect drug, concentration, or dose duration
hazardous_situation: Patient receives another patient's prescribed therapy or a mismatched drug protocol
harm: Wrong drug or dose administration; adverse event or treatment failure depending on drugs involved
risk_control: Barcode or NFC patient identification verification before therapy initiation; confirmation screen displaying patient name and MRN before start; DERS limits flagging incompatible therapy parameters; independent double-check workflow required for high-alert drug protocols
verification_method: Patient ID verification workflow testing; summative usability study including patient identification step; system integration testing with electronic prescribing system under simulated multi-patient scenarios

hazard: Loss of wireless communication between pump and clinical information or pharmacy system
sequence_of_events: Network outage or RF interference in clinical area → pump loses connectivity → remote dose change or stop order issued by pharmacist or physician not received by pump → pump continues at previous programming for extended period
hazardous_situation: Pump operates with outdated infusion parameters after a clinically significant remote order change
harm: Patient receives over- or under-dose relative to updated clinical order; risk depends on drug, time interval, and magnitude of discrepancy
risk_control: Audible and visual connectivity-loss indicator on pump display; infusion continues at last confirmed programming until clinician manually updates at bedside; remote order change requires bedside nurse acknowledgment before applying; connectivity monitoring with IT and clinical alert
verification_method: Communication failure mode simulation testing; dose update workflow validation in disconnected state; clinical simulation of remote dose change with connectivity loss; IT infrastructure monitoring validation

hazard: Electrochemical interference from maltose, galactose, or acetaminophen with glucose-oxidase enzyme system
sequence_of_events: Patient on peritoneal dialysis or receiving maltodextrin-containing fluid samples blood → interfering substance co-oxidised by enzyme → excess electrical signal generated → meter displays falsely elevated glucose reading
hazardous_situation: Patient believes glucose is elevated when actual glucose is within normal or low range
harm: Patient administers unnecessary insulin → iatrogenic hypoglycaemia; loss of consciousness, seizure, or death in severe hypoglycaemia
risk_control: Enzyme system with low-interference profile (FAD-GDH or equivalent) validated across interfering substances per ISO 15197 Annex C; labeling contraindication warning for incompatible medications and conditions; interference test results documented in IFU and clinical facility guidance
verification_method: Interference testing across common interferents per ISO 15197 Annex C; analytical accuracy study; clinical accuracy study; labeling review including contraindication completeness

hazard: Hematocrit effect causing systematic underestimation of glucose concentration
sequence_of_events: Patient with elevated hematocrit (polycythaemia, neonatal patient, dehydration) performs self-test → high hematocrit reduces rate of glucose diffusion and electrochemical signal → meter displays glucose significantly below actual value
hazardous_situation: Patient or caregiver accepts falsely normal glucose result while actual glucose is critically low
harm: Untreated hypoglycaemia resulting in neuroglycopenic injury, permanent neurological damage, or death
risk_control: Hematocrit correction algorithm validated across 20–60% Hct range per ISO 15197; out-of-range hematocrit detection flag blocking result if outside validated range; lot-release QC testing with Hct range acceptance criteria; clinical accuracy study including high-Hct patient groups
verification_method: Analytical accuracy testing per ISO 15197 across full validated hematocrit range; clinical performance study including paediatric population; lot release acceptance testing protocol review

hazard: Test strip enzymatic reagent degraded by humidity or temperature excursion outside specified storage range
sequence_of_events: User stores open strip vial in humid environment or exposes to extreme temperature → enzymatic reagent degrades → user tests glucose with degraded strip → meter produces unreliable reading without flagging an error to the user
hazardous_situation: Patient acts on glucose result from a chemically degraded test strip that is outside the device accuracy specification
harm: Treatment decision (insulin dose or carbohydrate intake) based on inaccurate glucose reading; hypoglycaemia or hyperglycaemia from inappropriate clinical response
risk_control: Strip stability testing across operating and storage temperature and humidity ranges; moisture-barrier vial cap design; in-use expiry period clearly stated on vial label; expiry date encoded in strip barcode for automated meter detection; storage condition warnings in IFU with user comprehension validated
verification_method: Environmental stability testing at temperature and humidity extremes; container moisture ingress testing; in-use stability study across vial open period; labeling review and user comprehension usability testing

hazard: Blood sample volume applied to test strip is below the validated minimum threshold
sequence_of_events: User obtains small capillary sample → test strip partially filled → electrochemical signal is incomplete or distorted → meter either displays erroneous result or error code that user overrides or ignores
hazardous_situation: Patient receives inaccurate glucose reading from an under-filled test strip and makes a dosing decision
harm: Incorrect insulin dose or carbohydrate intake based on invalid glucose result
risk_control: Fill detection algorithm blocking result display with mandatory error code if sample volume below validated minimum; test zone design requiring positive wicking confirmation signal; audible and visual fill confirmation feedback before measurement begins; IFU instruction with illustrated sample collection technique
verification_method: Minimum sample volume detection validation testing across sample volumes at and below threshold; fill detection algorithm performance validation; usability study evaluating sample collection technique with intended lay user population

hazard: Residual sugar or chemical substance on fingertip at capillary sampling site
sequence_of_events: Patient handles glucose gel, fruit, or juice before testing without washing hands → surface contamination mixed into capillary sample → apparent glucose far exceeds blood glucose → meter displays falsely elevated reading
hazardous_situation: Patient makes clinical decision based on a glucose reading substantially higher than actual blood glucose
harm: Patient administers excess insulin correction dose → severe iatrogenic hypoglycaemia; potentially fatal in elderly or diabetes patients on intensive insulin therapy
risk_control: IFU and quick-start guide explicitly instructing hand washing with soap and water before sampling; first-drop wipe step recommended in IFU before capillary sampling; graphical hand hygiene instruction on meter body or packaging; user training programme emphasising contamination risk with comprehension testing
verification_method: Contamination interference study simulating clinical contamination scenarios; usability evaluation with emphasis on hand hygiene step comprehension with lay users; labeling adequacy review for contamination warning prominence

hazard: Strip calibration code mismatch between meter setting and current test strip lot
sequence_of_events: User opens new strip lot with different calibration code → forgets to update code in meter manually → meter applies incorrect calibration factor to enzyme lot → systematic measurement bias applied to all results from new lot
hazardous_situation: All glucose readings from the new strip lot are systematically biased in one direction throughout its use period
harm: Persistent over- or under-treatment of diabetes based on systematically incorrect glucose results over days or weeks
risk_control: Automatic lot code recognition via barcode on vial or individual strip chip eliminating manual code entry; no-coding or auto-coding design as primary control; mismatch detection alarm if manual coding required; lot change UI prompt when new strip is first inserted
verification_method: Auto-coding functionality validation across multiple strip lots; code mismatch detection testing; analytical accuracy testing across multiple calibration code scenarios; labeling review

hazard: Ambient temperature outside validated device operating range during measurement
sequence_of_events: Patient performs test in cold outdoor environment or hot vehicle → ambient temperature outside operating specification → enzyme activity altered → electrochemical reaction rate deviates from calibration → meter reads outside accuracy tolerance
hazardous_situation: Patient receives glucose reading that is outside the device accuracy specification due to temperature excursion
harm: Treatment decision based on inaccurate result; hypoglycaemia or hyperglycaemia from incorrect clinical response
risk_control: Integrated temperature sensor with out-of-range flag displayed before result; validated operating range clearly stated on device body and IFU; storage and use temperature range prominently labelled; user guidance on warming meter and strips before use in cold conditions
verification_method: Temperature accuracy testing at lower and upper boundary conditions; temperature sensor validation; labeling review; usability testing of out-of-range temperature warning comprehension with lay users

hazard: Battery depletion mid-measurement without adequate prior low-battery warning
sequence_of_events: Patient initiates glucose test → battery capacity insufficient to complete full measurement cycle → meter powers off before displaying result → patient unable to determine glucose level
hazardous_situation: Patient cannot determine actual glucose level at the intended time of measurement and may be actively hypoglycaemic
harm: Delayed hypoglycaemia detection and treatment; loss of consciousness if patient is hypoglycaemic and unable to obtain a valid result and seek help
risk_control: Low battery warning displayed at ≥10 test doses remaining capacity; battery check performed at meter startup refusing to initiate measurement if capacity insufficient; IFU guidance on battery replacement frequency; battery life tested across operating temperature range
verification_method: Battery life testing at minimum operating voltage under full measurement load; low battery warning threshold validation; startup battery check refusal testing; environmental battery performance testing

hazard: Low display contrast or small digit size causing misreading of glucose result
sequence_of_events: Patient tests glucose in poorly lit room at night or in direct sunlight → display contrast insufficient for ambient conditions → patient misreads a digit (e.g., 4.0 misread as 1.0 mmol/L) → patient makes dosing decision based on misread value
hazardous_situation: Patient makes clinical decision based on a glucose value that is significantly different from what is actually displayed on the meter
harm: Inappropriate insulin dose or carbohydrate intake causing hypo- or hyperglycaemia
risk_control: Display minimum contrast ratio validated per IEC 62366 requirements; automatic backlight activation in low-light conditions; minimum digit height meeting legibility standards for intended user population; audio readout option for visually impaired users; units always displayed alongside result
verification_method: Display contrast ratio measurement at specified ambient light levels; display legibility usability testing with intended lay user population including elderly users; audio output validation; comprehension testing

hazard: Bluetooth data packet corruption or field mapping error during transmission to companion app
sequence_of_events: Meter performs measurement → Bluetooth data packet corrupted or field value incorrectly mapped during protocol transmission → app receives and displays different value from meter reading → patient or caregiver views app result without cross-checking meter display and makes treatment decision
hazardous_situation: Patient uses a transmitted glucose value from the app that differs from the actual measured result on the meter
harm: Inappropriate insulin dose or carbohydrate intake based on incorrect app-displayed value
risk_control: CRC or equivalent checksum validation on all transmitted glucose data packets; transmission error detected → app prompts user to re-check meter display before acting on result; Bluetooth specification compliance testing; independent audit of transmitted versus meter-displayed values in verification
verification_method: Data integrity testing under simulated transmission error conditions; Bluetooth stack protocol compliance validation; accuracy audit across 200 transmission cycles; app usability testing evaluating error handling behaviour

hazard: Glucose result unit confusion between mmol/L and mg/dL leading to treatment error
sequence_of_events: Patient accustomed to mg/dL units travels to country where meter default is mmol/L → meter displays 5.5 (mmol/L) → patient interprets reading as 5.5 mg/dL (critically hypoglycaemic equivalent) → patient consumes large carbohydrate load or fails to treat actual hypoglycaemia if units reversed
hazardous_situation: Patient applies 18-fold incorrect interpretation of glucose value due to unit system mismatch
harm: Severe hyperglycaemia from unnecessary large carbohydrate intake; or untreated hypoglycaemia if reversed unit interpretation
risk_control: Units (mmol/L or mg/dL) prominently displayed in large font alongside every glucose result; unit setting clearly labelled in device settings menu; audible or visual prompt requiring confirmation when unit setting is changed; IFU section on unit awareness with explicit warning for travellers; clinical guide on checking unit settings
verification_method: Display legibility testing for unit label; usability study with participants from both unit-system backgrounds; labeling review confirming unit display prominence; unit change confirmation workflow testing

hazard: Test strips used after printed expiry date or beyond open-vial in-use period
sequence_of_events: Patient uses strips from vial opened beyond 90-day in-use period or past printed expiry date → enzyme activity degraded → meter produces inaccurate result with no or dismissed error indication → patient makes treatment decision based on unreliable result
hazardous_situation: Patient obtains and acts upon a glucose reading from chemically degraded test strips
harm: Treatment decision based on unreliable glucose result; hypo- or hyperglycaemia from incorrect insulin or carbohydrate management
risk_control: Strip lot and expiry date encoded in barcode or chip; meter automatically reads strip expiry at strip insertion and displays warning or blocks measurement if expired or out-of-use-period; in-use expiry date reminder functionality after vial opening date recorded; prominently labelled expiry on vial
verification_method: Strip expiry detection and blocking functionality validation; analytical accuracy testing on strips at expiry boundary date; in-use stability testing at end of open-vial period; labeling review; usability testing of expiry warning comprehension and user response
